chunk_summary: This section outlines clinical markers to assess breastfeeding adequacy in infants 0–6 months, emphasizing weight trends, feeding mechanics, and early intervention to prevent complications like dehydration and failure to thrive.
learning_objectives: Identify clinical markers of adequate breastfeeding intake in infants 0–6 months.
Understand weight trajectory expectations and their role in assessing feeding adequacy.
Recognize signs of feeding difficulties and implement stepwise interventions.
Evaluate maternal and infant factors affecting breastfeeding success.
key_facts: By day 4–5, term infants should have ≥6 wet diapers per day and stools transitioning to yellow.
Term infants should regain birth weight by 10–14 days after initial weight loss.
Persistent sleepiness, brick-dust urate crystals after day 3, or inconsolable hunger are red flags.
Weight-for-age and weight-for-length trends are more critical than single data points.
Early intervention includes optimizing latch, increasing feeding frequency, and reassessing within 24–48 hours.
Failure to address feeding issues can lead to hyperbilirubinemia, hypoglycemia, and failure to thrive.
reference_quote: ‘Track weight on the same scale when possible. Some early weight loss is expected, but a term infant should start gaining once lactogenesis is established and should typically regain birth weight by 10–14 days.’ (American Academy of Pediatrics)
document_key_topics: Signs of feeding difficulties and interventions
Prevention of complications like dehydration and failure to thrive
